title: Yahweh: Hearing, Seeing, Remembering, and Knowing
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: 02-11-24 -PM- Yahweh_ Hearing, Seeing, Remembering, and Knowing - Exod 2_23-25 - Elder Scott Hollander.txt

outline.title: Introduction: God's Immanence — Hearing, Seeing, Remembering, and Knowing
subpoints: Preliminary text: Exodus 2:23-25 — God heard, remembered, looked upon, and acknowledged the Israelites in bondage
Context: Moses' flight from Egypt, 40 years in the wilderness, right before the burning bush
The theme: Yahweh's immanence — His presence with and involvement in the lives of His covenantal people
God's immutability: He relates to His people the same way today — weaving blessing and suffering into the covenantal story of each life

outline.title: Illustration 1: The Israelites Learn That God Hears, Remembers, Sees, and Knows
subpoints: Exodus 3:7: 'I have surely seen the oppression of My people... and have heard their cry... for I know their sorrows'
Exodus 4:31: When Moses and Aaron told the people, they bowed and worshiped — because they heard that the Lord had looked on their affliction
Exodus 6:5-8: 'I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel... I have remembered My covenant... I am Yahweh your God'

outline.title: Illustration 2: Hagar Sees That God Hears and Sees (Genesis 16)
subpoints: Hagar fled into the wilderness after Sarah's harsh treatment — lost, afraid, alone
The Angel of the Lord (a Christophany) met her with comfort and a promise
The child's name: Ishmael — 'God hears' — because the Lord heard her affliction
Hagar named God: 'You are the God who sees' — Beer Lahai Roi = 'the well of the living one who sees me'
Though a servant and an outsider, Hagar was part of the covenant community — and God met her in her despair

outline.title: Illustration 3: Yahweh Sees and Hears Leah and Rachel (Genesis 29-30)
subpoints: Leah was unloved; God opened her womb and blessed her
Her sons' names encode God's response: Reuben = 'see a son' ('the Lord has looked upon my affliction'); Simeon = 'heard' ('the Lord heard that I am unloved')
Rachel was beloved but barren; 'God has judged my case and has also heard my voice' — her son Dan = 'judge'
Both women's children were part of the covenantal fulfillment of God's promises to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob

outline.title: Illustration 4: Yahweh Remembers and Hears Hannah (1 Samuel 1)
subpoints: 'The Lord remembered her' — 1 Samuel 1:19-20
Hannah's son Samuel = Shemuel = 'heard by God' — she named him for the hearing of the Lord
Our prayers may be like Hannah's — groaning, weeping — but our God is the God of hearing, seeing, remembering, and knowing
Romans 8:26-27: the Holy Spirit intercedes for us with groanings that cannot be uttered when we don't know what to pray

outline.title: Application: God Is the Same Today
subpoints: God's immutability means He continues to act the same way toward His covenantal people today
He knows your blessings and your suffering and is weaving them into the covenantal story of your life
Psalm 84:11: 'No good thing does He withhold from those who walk uprightly'; Romans 8:28
James 5:16: the effective, fervent prayer of a righteous man avails much — bring your prayers to the God who sees, hears, remembers, and knows
Response throughout Scripture: gratitude and worship — Hagar, Leah, Rachel, and Hannah each responded with praise

summary: Elder Scott Hollander traces the Old Testament theme of Yahweh as a hearing, seeing, remembering, and knowing God through four narrative illustrations: the Israelites in Egyptian bondage (Exod 2-6), Hagar in the wilderness (Gen 16), Leah and Rachel (Gen 29-30), and Hannah (1 Sam 1). He notes that the meanings of the names in each narrative encode God's response to His people's cries, and that God's immutability means He continues to see, hear, remember, and know His covenantal people today. The Holy Spirit intercedes for believers when they cannot find words to pray (Rom 8:26-27), and the proper response to this God is gratitude and worship.
transcript:
## Introduction: The God Who Is Present

I love studying Scripture, and most fundamentally because it is the word that reveals the gospel message of how God reconciled sinful man to Himself. But also the more one reads it, the more you learn about the attributes of God throughout Scripture — the multiple themes of His working in and through His people.

This afternoon I want to explore one of those themes: God as the God of hearing, seeing, remembering, and knowing.

Our preliminary passage is Exodus 2:23-25:

> "Now it happened in the process of time that the king of Egypt died. Then the children of Israel groaned because of the bondage, and they cried out; and their cry came up to God because of the bondage. So God heard their groaning, and God remembered His covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob. And God looked upon the children of Israel, and God acknowledged them."

The context is the period following Moses's flight from Egypt — right before the burning bush narrative. Moses has spent forty years in the wilderness tending the sheep of his father-in-law. God had forewarned Abraham in Genesis 15 that his descendants would be afflicted in a strange land for four hundred years. That time of testing was approaching its end.

What we primarily learn about God from these verses: Yahweh is a **hearing** God, a **remembering** God, a **seeing** God, and a **knowing** God. These are illustrations of His *immanence* — His presence with and involvement in the lives of His covenantal people.

Before we proceed: remember God's *immutability*. God does not change. What I am about to describe is not how God used to act in the Old Testament only. He continues to act this same way today. He knows your blessings and your suffering and is weaving them into the covenantal story of your life. Psalm 84:11: *No good thing does He withhold from those who walk uprightly.* Romans 8:28: *All things work together for good to those who love God.*

## 1. The Israelites: God Hears, Remembers, Sees, and Knows

In Exodus 3:7, before God reveals His eternal name, He tells Moses:

> "I have surely seen the oppression of My people who are in Egypt, and have heard their cry because of their taskmasters, for I know their sorrows."

In Exodus 4:31, when Moses and Aaron relayed God's message to the people:

> "They bowed their heads and worshiped" — because they heard that the Lord had visited the children of Israel and had looked on their affliction.

In Exodus 6:5-8, God reassures Moses with the full weight of His covenant:

> "I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel whom the Egyptians keep in bondage, and I have remembered My covenant... I am the LORD. I will bring you out... I will rescue you... I will redeem you... I will take you as My people and I will be your God."

Over and over through the Exodus narrative, Yahweh speaks covenantal language: *I am your God. We have a binding relationship. I see you. I hear you. I remember you. I know you and the suffering you are experiencing.*

## 2. Hagar: God Hears and Sees (Genesis 16)

In Genesis 16, after Abraham failed to trust God and fathered a son through Hagar — and Sarah treated Hagar harshly — Hagar fled into the wilderness. She was lost, afraid, and alone.

The Angel of the Lord met her:

> "The Angel of the LORD said to her: 'Behold, you are with child, and you shall bear a son. You shall call his name Ishmael' — which in Hebrew means *God hears* — 'because the LORD has heard your affliction.'" (Genesis 16:11)

Hagar responded by naming God:

> "She called the name of the LORD who spoke to her, 'You are the God who sees'; for she said, 'Have I also here seen Him who sees me?' Therefore the well was called Beer Lahai Roi" — which in Hebrew means *the well of the living one who sees me.*

Hagar was a servant — an outsider, a Gentile. Yet as a member of the covenant community, she was seen and heard by God in her despair. He met her need with comfort, guidance, and promise. Her response was gratitude and worship.

## 3. Leah and Rachel: God Sees and Hears (Genesis 29-30)

Genesis 29:31:

> "When the LORD saw that Leah was unloved, He opened her womb... So Leah conceived and bore a son, and she called his name Reuben" — which in Hebrew means *see a son* — "for she said, 'The LORD has surely looked upon my affliction.'" Then her second son she called *Simeon* — which means *heard* — "because the LORD has heard that I am unloved."

And in Genesis 30:6, Rachel:

> "God has judged my case, and He has also heard my voice and given me a son. Therefore she called his name Dan" — which in Hebrew means *judge.*

Both Leah and Rachel were covenanted to Jacob, whose children were in direct fulfillment of God's promises to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. In their loneliness and barrenness, God saw and heard them — and wove their suffering into the tapestry of His redemptive plan.

## 4. Hannah: God Remembers and Hears (1 Samuel 1)

1 Samuel 1:19-20:

> "Elkanah knew Hannah his wife, and the LORD remembered her. So it came to pass in the process of time that Hannah conceived and bore a son, and called his name Samuel" — Shemuel in Hebrew, meaning *heard by God* — "saying, 'Because I have asked for him from the LORD.'"

Even in the naming of Samuel, the hearing of God is encoded. Hannah's prayer was offered in tears, in groaning, in words so private that Eli thought she was drunk. But God heard. And He acted.

## Application: The God Who Hears, Sees, Remembers, and Knows You

Our God is the same yesterday, today, and forever. The Author of Hebrews says so directly (Heb. 13:8). The hearing, seeing, remembering, and knowing God of the Old Testament — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — is your God today.

In your suffering, you may not know what to pray. Romans 8:26-27:

> "The Spirit also helps in our weaknesses. For we do not know what we should pray for as we ought, but the Spirit Himself makes intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered. Now He who searches the hearts knows what the mind of the Spirit is, because He makes intercession for the saints according to the will of God."

James 5:16: *The effective, fervent prayer of a righteous man avails much.*

Bring your prayers to this God. He sees you where you are. He hears your prayers. He remembers the covenant He made with you. And He knows you. The response throughout Scripture — from Hagar, from Leah and Rachel, from Hannah — was gratitude and worship. May it be ours as well.
